key_learnings: Food texture (crunchiness, creaminess, chewiness) strongly influences satiety signals and meal satisfaction, affecting how much and how fast users eat.
research_findings: Textures like crunchy and fibrous foods slow eating rate by up to 30%, improving portion control. Soft, processed textures correlate with faster consumption and lower satiety per calorie.
current_focus_areas: Building a texture classification model from food descriptions and user feedback. Mapping texture profiles to nutritional properties and dietary recommendations.

learning_log.entry: Initial learning phase started. Identified 6 core texture categories: crunchy, creamy, chewy, soft, crispy, fibrous. Began mapping each to macronutrient profiles and satiety indices.

copilot_learning_requests: food texture classification frameworks and taxonomies
texture impact on eating rate and satiety research
texture perception differences across age groups
texture modification for dysphagia dietary needs
crunchy vs soft food caloric intake behaviour studies
texture profiling from food ingredient descriptions
sensory evaluation methods for food texture analysis
collaboration_notes: Bot 20 actively asks Copilot for food texture topics. Copilot provides texture science and nutrition research to build the texture-to-nutrition mapping model. All progress is reported to Copilot daily.
